channel.title: Opportunities for Youth
channel.description: Empowering Youth through Access to Information and Opportunities

item.title: OECD Research Fellowships Research Fellowships 2027 | Fully Funded International Research Fellowships for Agricultural, Forestry & Fisheries Scientists
item.dc:creator: admin
item.description:
CRP Applications for Fellowships Applications are now open for the OECD Co-operative Research Programme (CRP) Research Fellowships 2027, offering scientists the opportunity to undertake 6–26 weeks of funded international research in another participating country. The programme supports cutting-edge research that advances sustainable agriculture, forestry, fisheries, food systems, climate resilience and natural resource management while fostering […]

The post OECD Research Fellowships Research Fellowships 2027 | Fully Funded International Research Fellowships for Agricultural, Forestry & Fisheries Scientists appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
CRP Applications for Fellowships

Applications are now open for the OECD Co-operative Research Programme (CRP) Research Fellowships 2027, offering scientists the opportunity to undertake 6–26 weeks of funded international research in another participating country. The programme supports cutting-edge research that advances sustainable agriculture, forestry, fisheries, food systems, climate resilience and natural resource management while fostering global scientific collaboration.

Key Details

  • Host Organisation: OECD Co-operative Research Programme (CRP)
  • Programme: CRP Research Fellowships 2027
  • Funding: Travel grant including return economy airfare, €600–€650 weekly subsistence allowance, and a €165 terminal allowance
  • Duration: 6–26 weeks
  • Eligible Applicants: Researchers working in agriculture, forestry or fisheries with at least four years of postdoctoral experience (or equivalent scientific expertise in exceptional cases). The programme is not intended for PhD students.
  • Research Location: Another participating OECD CRP member country
  • Eligible Countries: Researchers based in participating CRP countries, including the United Kingdom, United States, Canada, Australia, Brazil, Japan, Germany, France, Netherlands, Sweden, Switzerland, Spain, Ireland, New Zealand and others.
  • Research Areas: Sustainable agriculture, food security, climate change, biodiversity, forestry, fisheries, aquaculture, soil health, livestock systems, ecosystem services, Indigenous knowledge, water quality and agricultural innovation.
  • Application Deadline: 10 September 2026 (Midnight, Paris Time)
  • Results Announced: January 2027
  • Fellowship Start: From March 2027
  • Application Fee: None

Application process for fellowships

The  Co-operative Research Programme’s call for applications for funding research fellowship grants in 2027 is now OPEN.

The deadline to apply is Thursday 10 September 2026, midnight, Paris time.

General information

> Applicants must be residents in one of the countries participating in our Programme.  Please consult the files for general information on the country’s research institutes and universities.

Please note that:

  • Türkiye is a member until 24 December 2026 and will not participate in the call for applications for events and fellowships to be funded in 2027.
  • Brazil is a member of the CRP as of 1 July 2026, so Brazil is eligible to the call for applications for fellowships to be funded in 2027.
  • Ukraine is on ACCESSION STAGE. Should you want to have speakers from this country, please contact TAD.PROG@oecd.org to be updated on its status.

> Please consult the Event Guidelines and Conditions, Selection Criteria and the CRP Action Areas in the documents below.

> Applications relevant to the work of the OECD Committee for Agriculture and other bodies are particularly welcome:

• Sustainable and resilient productivity growth and food security and nutrition
• Climate risks, reducing emissions from agriculture and food systems, carbon sequestration in agriculture, forestry and land use
• Halting and reversing forest loss and land degradation
• Reducing the negative environmental impacts of livestock production and practices harmful to animal health and welfare, investigating the positive contribution livestock can make to soil quality and management, biodiversity and livelihoods
• Biodiversity, enhancing ecosystem services
• Improving soil health and water and air quality, including through agro-ecological and other innovative, context specific approaches
• Innovations in the transfer and development of agricultural knowledge, including Indigenous and traditional knowledge
• Fisheries and aquaculture productivity, sustainability and resilience

> Previous research fellowships are available for review. Testimonials from past fellows and their hosts can also be reviewed on the CRP home page (scroll down to the dedicated section).

Eligibility Requirements

Beyond being a researcher in agriculture, forestry or fisheries, applicants should also note that they must:

  • Be employed at an institution located in a participating CRP country.
  • Undertake the fellowship in another participating CRP country.
  • Normally have at least four years of postdoctoral experience. The programme is not intended for PhD students, although exceptional applicants without a PhD but with equivalent expertise and a strong publication record may be considered.
  • Have employer approval before submitting the application.
  • Have continued employment (or ongoing scientific affiliation) after the fellowship ends.
  • Previous CRP fellows may only apply again five years after receiving their first fellowship.

What Does the Fellowship Cover?

The OECD CRP Fellowship provides a travel lump-sum allowance consisting of three components:

  • Return economy-class travel between the fellow’s home institution and host institution
  • Weekly subsistence allowance of €600–€650 per week (depending on the cost of living in the host country)
  • A €165 terminal allowance to help cover transport costs at the beginning and end of the fellowship

What is Not Covered?

Applicants should budget separately for:

  • Medical and travel insurance
  • Visa and passport costs
  • Laboratory or bench fees
  • Daily commuting costs (covered within the subsistence allowance)
  • Travel or expenses for accompanying family members
  • Taxes and bank charges where applicable

To apply for a research fellowship award

1. please consult the following documents:

  • Guidelines and Conditions for CRP Research Fellowship Awards
  • Selection Criteria for CRP Fellowship Awards
  • Action Areas of the Co-operative Research Programme
  • Sample fellowship applications: Example 1; Example 2
  • Sample CV
  • Sample invitation letter
  • PDF Fellowship Application form

2. complete the following document:

  • Online application form for CRP Research Fellowships 

National correspondents and country files

The Co-operative Research Programme (CRP) currently has 32 participating countries with Brazil joining on 1 July 2026. Below is a list of countries, the contact information for their national correspondents and when available a [Country file], document which gives general information on the country’s research institutes and universities.

  • Australia                  Dr. Andy SHEPPARD
  • Austria                     Ms. Sanda PASC-WANDL                     [Country file]
  • Belgium                  Mme Véronique DEWASMES              [Country file]
  • Brazil                        Dr. Maria José A. M. SAMPAIO            [Country file]
  • Canada                    Ms Melanie SINGARAYER
  • Chile                         Mr Jack BRADY                                        [Country file]
  • Colombia                Mr. Felipe CORREAL CHARRIS
  • Costa Rica               Mr. José Fabián JIMÉNEZ MORALES
  • Czechia                    Ms. Šárka ŠTEJNAROVÁ                        [Country file]
  • Denmark                 Mr. Bjarne THOMSEN
  • Estonia                     Dr. Katri LING                                          [Country file]
  • Finland                     Prof. Pirjo PELTONEN-SAINIO            [Country file]
  • France                      Dr. Joachim HUET
  • Germany                 Prof. Dr. Jens DAUBER
  • Hungary                  Prof. András SZÉKÁCS
  • Ireland                     Ms. Ellen DURKIN
  • Israel                        Dr. Michal LEVY                                        [Country file]
  • Italy                          Ms. Silvia BARALLA
  • Japan                        Dr. Ichiro NAKAYAMA
  • Korea                        Ms Da-Som KANG
  • Latvia                        Ms Diāna LĪVA                                         [Country file]
  • Lithuania                 Ms Vilma KRAUJALYTE                           [Country file]
  • Netherlands           Ms. Saske HOVING                                 [Country file]
  • New Zealand          Ms. Shania BROOKS                               [Country file]
  • Norway                    Dr. Maan Singh SIDHU                          [Country file]
  • Slovak Republic     Dr. Kristína GENDOVÁ RUZSÍKOVÁ     [Country file]
  • Spain                        Dr. Violeta CARRASCO
  • Sweden                    Dr. Sara GRÄSLUND                               [Country file]
  • Switzerland             Ms Veronica CAGGIA
  • Türkiye                     Dr. Fatih ÖZDEMİR
  • United Kingdom    Mr. Alen BIJU                                            [Country file]
  • United States          Ms. Marcella WITTING

How Applications Are Evaluated

Applications are assessed against several selection criteria. Strong proposals should clearly demonstrate:

  • Scientific excellence and innovation
  • Relevance to the OECD CRP Action Areas
  • A feasible and well-designed research plan
  • Long-term collaboration between the applicant and host institution
  • Strong scientific achievements and publication record
  • Multidisciplinary approaches
  • Plans for dissemination of research findings
  • Practical impact of the research
  • Clear policy relevance for agriculture, forestry or fisheries

New international collaborations are given preference over existing collaborations

Application Requirements

Applicants must submit:

  • Completed online application form
  • Curriculum Vitae (CV)
  • List of publications from the past five years
  • Signed invitation letter from the host institution
  • Research proposal addressing all selection criteria

Applications must be submitted in English.

Fellowship Timeline

  • Applications close: 10 September 2026
  • Award decisions: Mid-December 2026
  • Successful applicants notified: By January 2027
  • Recommended fellowship start: From 1 March 2027
  • Latest fellowship departure: 15 December 2027

Responsibilities of Successful Fellows

Recipients of the fellowship will be expected to:

  • Confirm arrival at the host institution
  • Complete an evaluation questionnaire after the fellowship
  • Submit a summary report within two months of completing the research
  • Acknowledge OECD CRP funding in any publications arising from the fellowship

Failure to submit the required final report may result in repayment of 10% of the fellowship award.

Contact

For any question regarding a fellowship application, please contact the CRP Secretariat: TAD.Prog@oecd.org

Source: https://www.oecd.org/en/about/programmes/co-operative-research-programme/crp-applications-for-fellowships.html

The post OECD Research Fellowships Research Fellowships 2027 | Fully Funded International Research Fellowships for Agricultural, Forestry & Fisheries Scientists appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: ASIF Ignite Africa Cohort 1 2026: 8-Week Remote Accelerator for African Climate-Tech Startups Building Food & Agriculture Solutions
item.dc:creator: Stalin Daniel
item.description:
Applications are now open for the ASIF Ignite Africa Cohort 1, an intensive 8-week fully remote venture support programme designed specifically for early-stage founders developing climate adaptation and resilience solutions across Africa’s food and agriculture value chains. The programme supports founders who have already built a working prototype and are ready to validate their ideas […]

The post ASIF Ignite Africa Cohort 1 2026: 8-Week Remote Accelerator for African Climate-Tech Startups Building Food & Agriculture Solutions appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications are now open for the ASIF Ignite Africa Cohort 1, an intensive 8-week fully remote venture support programme designed specifically for early-stage founders developing climate adaptation and resilience solutions across Africa’s food and agriculture value chains.

The programme supports founders who have already built a working prototype and are ready to validate their ideas with real users. Rather than focusing on theoretical business planning, Ignite helps startups move from assumptions to evidence by testing products in real-world environments, gathering customer feedback, refining business models, and preparing ventures for future investment and growth.

Unlike many startup accelerators that focus primarily on scaling businesses, ASIF Ignite places climate resilience at the center of innovation. The programme supports solutions that help vulnerable communities, especially smallholder farmers, better prepare for, withstand, recover from, and adapt to the growing impacts of climate change.

Applications remain open until 17 August 2026.

About ASIF Ignite Africa

ASIF Ignite is a cohort-based venture development programme that brings together promising early-stage founders from across Africa to validate climate adaptation innovations.

The programme is designed for entrepreneurs who have already moved beyond the idea stage by building a tangible product, service, technology, application, platform, device, or operating model, but who have not yet tested it extensively with their intended users.

Throughout the eight-week programme, participants receive structured guidance to:

  • Validate customer demand
  • Conduct real-world testing
  • Learn Lean Startup methodologies
  • Explore practical AI applications
  • Build measurable evidence of impact
  • Improve investor readiness
  • Strengthen business models

The programme is delivered entirely online, making it accessible to founders across Africa.

Why This Programme Matters

Climate change continues to place enormous pressure on Africa’s food systems.

Smallholder farmers increasingly face:

  • Droughts
  • Unpredictable rainfall
  • Flooding
  • Soil degradation
  • Crop losses
  • Poor market access
  • Rising temperatures
  • Food spoilage
  • Supply chain disruptions

Across Sub-Saharan Africa, up to one-third of food produced is lost before reaching consumers due to inadequate storage, poor logistics, and climate-related disruptions.

ASIF Ignite seeks founders developing practical innovations that improve resilience throughout the agricultural value chain.

Programme Focus for Africa Cohort 1

The first African cohort specifically focuses on food and agriculture value chains.

This includes every stage involved in producing food, including:

  • Farm inputs
  • Production
  • Water management
  • Farm technologies
  • Storage
  • Transportation
  • Processing
  • Distribution
  • Market access

The programme supports solutions that enable farmers and agribusinesses to prepare for climate shocks while maintaining sustainable livelihoods.

Solution Areas Supported

The programme welcomes innovations across multiple areas.

1. Inputs and Agricultural Knowledge

Solutions that improve farmers’ access to critical resources and information, including:

  • Climate-resilient seeds
  • Improved planting materials
  • Farm machinery
  • Irrigation systems
  • Greenhouse technologies
  • Agricultural input financing
  • Input logistics
  • Supply chain innovations
  • Weather-index insurance
  • Microcredit
  • Climate forecasting
  • Early warning systems
  • Agronomic advisory services
  • Solar-powered farm energy
  • Energy-efficient farming technologies

2. Growing Crops More Sustainably

Innovations that help farmers produce more while using fewer resources.

Examples include:

  • Hydroponics
  • Aquaponics
  • Solar irrigation
  • Farm management software
  • Controlled-environment agriculture
  • Remote sensing
  • Agricultural drones
  • Smart sensors
  • Farm automation technologies

3. Protecting Land and Water

Solutions that strengthen natural ecosystems and long-term agricultural productivity.

Examples include:

  • Sustainable fisheries
  • Fishery management
  • Agroforestry
  • Land restoration
  • Regenerative agriculture
  • Soil health improvement

4. Post-Harvest Management

Technologies that reduce food loss after harvesting.

Examples include:

  • Cold storage
  • Refrigeration
  • Warehousing
  • Aggregation centres
  • Agricultural hubs
  • Market access platforms
  • Trading platforms

5. Processing and Market Access

The programme also welcomes processing and sales innovations when they directly improve climate resilience for smallholder farmers.

Examples include:

  • Solar-powered milling
  • Low-energy food processing
  • Distribution systems
  • Market access technologies

Processing solutions that only improve operational efficiency without benefiting smallholder farmers are not eligible.

AI is Encouraged but Not Required

One unique aspect of ASIF Ignite is its practical approach to Artificial Intelligence.

Applicants do not need prior AI knowledge.

Instead, founders are encouraged to learn how AI can strengthen existing products.

Possible AI applications include:

  • Satellite data analysis
  • Crop disease detection
  • Credit scoring for farmers
  • Weather forecasting
  • Personalized farming advice
  • Automated insurance claims
  • Local-language agricultural advisory services

Whether AI becomes the main product or simply enhances an existing solution, participants receive practical guidance on integrating it effectively.

Who Should Apply?

The programme is open to:

  • Early-stage startups
  • MSMEs
  • Founder-led ventures
  • Social enterprises
  • Climate technology entrepreneurs

Applicants should:

  • Have developed a functional prototype
  • Be preparing for real-world testing
  • Focus on climate adaptation
  • Operate within food and agriculture value chains
  • Have or be building a sustainable revenue model

Businesses primarily dependent on grants or donations are not eligible.

Company registration is optional during application but should be completed before programme completion whenever possible.

What Stage Should Your Startup Be?

Ideal applicants should have already built a prototype.

Examples include:

  • A solar-powered cold room ready for market testing.
  • A weather advisory application ready for use by farmers.
  • A satellite-based credit scoring platform prepared for pilot deployment.

Solutions should have progressed beyond laboratory demonstrations and be ready for testing with actual users.

Who Is Not Eligible?

The following are generally not suitable:

  • Businesses without climate adaptation relevance.
  • Projects focused solely on reducing emissions without helping communities adapt.
  • Products designed only to increase yields without improving resilience.
  • Traditional processing businesses with no benefit to smallholder farmers.
  • Large corporations with mature commercial products.
  • Businesses operating outside food and agriculture.
  • Processing or sales businesses that improve efficiency alone without strengthening farmer resilience.

What Participants Receive

Selected founders will gain access to an extensive support package including:

  • Weekly live workshops
  • Customer discovery training
  • Lean experimentation methods
  • AI implementation guidance
  • Impact measurement support
  • One-on-one mentoring
  • Office hours with AI specialists
  • Product experts
  • Agricultural sector advisors
  • Temporary access to premium digital tools
  • Playbooks for testing solutions in climate-vulnerable communities
  • Feedback from investors and industry experts

Outcomes After Graduation

By the end of the programme, founders will leave with:

  • A stronger business model
  • AI-enabled product improvements
  • Real customer validation
  • Evidence of market demand
  • Measurable impact data
  • Improved investor readiness
  • A refined pitch for future funding opportunities

Outstanding ventures may be identified for future Propel cohorts, although graduation from Ignite does not guarantee automatic progression.

Programme Format

The accelerator consists of:

  • 8-week programme
  • Fully remote delivery
  • Approximately 13–14 founders per cohort
  • Two live workshop sessions every week
  • Additional asynchronous assignments between sessions

To support founders balancing work or caregiving responsibilities, workshop content can be recorded for later viewing.

Programme Timeline

Months 1–3

  • Applications
  • Review process
  • Cohort selection

Months 4–5

  • Onboarding
  • Workshops
  • AI learning
  • Mentorship
  • Product development

Months 5–6

  • Validation
  • Evidence building
  • Showcase
  • Graduation
  • Expert feedback

Important Dates

Application Deadline: 17 August 2026

Why You Should Apply

If you are building a climate innovation capable of helping African farmers adapt to climate change, this programme provides a rare opportunity to validate your solution with expert guidance before scaling.

Instead of simply teaching entrepreneurship, ASIF Ignite helps founders generate the evidence investors, customers, and partners want to see while strengthening products through customer feedback and practical AI integration.

Founders who complete the programme will be better positioned to attract funding, scale responsibly, and deliver measurable climate resilience across African food systems.

How to Apply

Interested applicants can learn more and apply for the ASIF Ignite Africa Cohort 1 2026 HERE.

Explore more global job opportunities on OFY here.

The post ASIF Ignite Africa Cohort 1 2026: 8-Week Remote Accelerator for African Climate-Tech Startups Building Food & Agriculture Solutions appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: PILnet-RLRH Fellowship 2026: Fully Remote International Legal Fellowship Supporting Refugee Leaders | Up to 12 Months of Professional Development and Public Interest Law Experience
item.dc:creator: Stalin Daniel
item.description:
The PILnet-RLRH Fellowship 2026 presents an outstanding opportunity for emerging legal professionals and refugee leaders to gain hands-on experience in public interest law while contributing to one of the world’s most impactful initiatives supporting refugee-led organizations. Organized through a partnership between PILnet and the Refugee-Led Research Hub (RLRH) at the Refugee Studies Centre, University of […]

The post PILnet-RLRH Fellowship 2026: Fully Remote International Legal Fellowship Supporting Refugee Leaders | Up to 12 Months of Professional Development and Public Interest Law Experience appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The PILnet-RLRH Fellowship 2026 presents an outstanding opportunity for emerging legal professionals and refugee leaders to gain hands-on experience in public interest law while contributing to one of the world’s most impactful initiatives supporting refugee-led organizations.

Organized through a partnership between PILnet and the Refugee-Led Research Hub (RLRH) at the Refugee Studies Centre, University of Oxford, this international remote fellowship is designed to empower individuals whose lived experiences and legal expertise can help transform access to justice for displaced communities around the world.

The fellowship offers participants the opportunity to conduct meaningful legal research, influence refugee policy discussions, strengthen professional leadership skills, and collaborate with internationally recognized experts working on forced displacement and refugee rights.

Applications are currently open, and interested candidates are encouraged to apply before the 15 July 2026 deadline.

About the PILnet-RLRH Fellowship

The PILnet-RLRH Fellowship was established to address an important gap within the legal and civil society sectors.

Many talented refugee leaders and professionals with lived experience of displacement possess valuable knowledge but often face barriers to accessing leadership opportunities, professional networks, mentorship, and career advancement.

This fellowship recognizes that lived experience is a powerful form of expertise.

Rather than viewing displaced persons solely as beneficiaries, the fellowship positions them as researchers, legal professionals, advocates, and leaders capable of shaping international conversations surrounding refugee protection and access to justice.

Successful fellows will follow the path of previous fellows such as Lidya Tewelde Habtay and Salah Jawish, contributing to PILnet’s growing work supporting refugee-led organizations globally.

Fellowship Overview

Organization

  • PILnet

Partner Organization

  • Refugee-Led Research Hub (RLRH), Refugee Studies Centre, University of Oxford

Position

  • PILnet-RLRH Legal Fellow

Location

  • Fully Remote

Applicants must remain based in:

  • Africa
  • Europe
  • The Americas

Duration

  • Up to 12 months

Work Schedule

  • 20–24 hours per week
  • Approximately 3–4 working days weekly

Working schedules will be agreed upon at the beginning of the fellowship.

Start Date

  • Flexible
  • Expected from August 2026

Earlier or later start dates may be arranged depending on the fellow’s availability.

Financial Support

  • Part-time stipend of £540 (subject to adjustment depending on location).

Application Deadline

Deadline: 15 July 2026

Applicants will receive notification of the outcome approximately 3–4 weeks after the application deadline.

Fellowship Objectives

The fellowship seeks to develop future leaders within refugee advocacy by focusing on several important objectives:

  • Center lived experience alongside professional legal expertise.
  • Strengthen leadership and strategic thinking.
  • Expand professional mentoring opportunities.
  • Build lasting international professional networks.
  • Increase participation of refugee leaders in legal research and advocacy.
  • Support PILnet’s forced displacement initiatives.
  • Improve access to justice for refugee-led organizations.

Fellowship Responsibilities

Participants will work across four major areas throughout the fellowship.

1. Mentorship and Professional Development

Each fellow will:

  • Develop an Individual Development Plan.
  • Work closely with mentors.
  • Receive ongoing guidance throughout the fellowship.
  • Build long-term leadership skills.

2. Lead an International Legal Research Project

The fellow will lead a significant research project expanding previous work conducted by former PILnet-RLRH fellows.

The research focuses on understanding the legal needs of Refugee-Led Organizations (RLOs).

Previous studies gathered evidence from:

  • Uganda
  • Egypt
  • South Africa
  • Australia

alongside NGO representatives, academic experts, and PILnet’s Refugee-Led Clearinghouse data collected between 2020 and 2024.

The 2026 fellow will expand this work by including perspectives from the Americas, filling an important geographical gap identified in earlier research.

Main Research Objectives

The project includes two major goals:

1. Update Existing Research

The fellow will:

  • Review previous findings.
  • Incorporate new legal developments.
  • Analyze updated Refugee-Led Clearinghouse data.
  • Integrate lessons learned since the original publication.

2. Expand Research to the Americas

The fellow will:

  • Conduct primary research with Refugee-LLed Organizations.
  • Interview refugee leaders.
  • Engage NGOs.
  • Consult legal practitioners.
  • Produce updated comparative global analysis.

Research Activities

The fellow is expected to:

  • Conduct semi-structured interviews.
  • Collect qualitative research.
  • Analyze legal needs affecting refugee-led organizations.
  • Produce an updated international report.
  • Prepare additional legal tools or policy briefs where appropriate.
  • Coordinate closely with PILnet staff and the Refugee-Led Research Hub.

Professional Development Opportunities

Beyond research, fellows will benefit from:

  • Leadership development.
  • Professional mentoring.
  • International networking.
  • Workshops.
  • Events.
  • Specialized training.
  • Collaboration with global legal professionals.
  • Exposure to public interest law practice.

Organizational Contributions

Throughout the fellowship, participants will also contribute to:

  • PILnet’s Refugee Clearinghouse.
  • Discussions surrounding forced displacement programming.
  • Strategic initiatives related to refugee access to justice.
  • Cross-organizational collaboration.

Fellowship Deliverables

Participants will complete several outputs during the fellowship, including:

  • Individual Development Plan
  • Agreed research deliverable
  • Mid-program reflection
  • Final presentation
  • Written reflection report

Expected Learning Outcomes

By the end of the fellowship, participants will have:

  • Stronger leadership capacity.
  • Improved strategic and programmatic thinking.
  • Greater understanding of forced displacement issues.
  • Advanced knowledge of refugee legal needs.
  • Practical experience in public interest law.
  • Enhanced skills in pro bono partnership building.
  • Expanded global professional networks.
  • Experience producing high-quality legal research.

Eligibility Requirements

Applicants should meet the following criteria:

Essential Requirements

  • Lived experience of forced displacement or current/former Mastercard Foundation Scholar.
  • Undergraduate or graduate legal degree, or significant legal experience.
  • Active leadership in Refugee-Led Organizations or refugee advocacy.
  • Demonstrated impact on refugee-related policies, programs, or practices.
  • Excellent written and spoken English.
  • Strong collaboration skills.

Preferred Qualifications

  • Knowledge of the global refugee protection system.
  • Additional language proficiency.

About PILnet

PILnet is an international non-governmental organization that partners with legal professionals and civil society organizations worldwide to strengthen justice systems and civic participation.

Its work focuses on three strategic priorities:

  • Civic Space
  • Climate Justice
  • Forced Displacement

Through partnerships, legal expertise, and professional development, PILnet works toward building more equitable, inclusive, and sustainable societies.

About the Refugee-Led Research Hub (RLRH)

The Refugee-Led Research Hub supports individuals with lived experience of displacement to become leaders across multiple disciplines, including:

  • Law
  • Human Rights
  • Politics
  • Forced Migration
  • Research
  • Public Policy

Since its establishment, the organization has built an international community of more than 1,400 researchers, students, and professionals across over 100 countries.

Its active offices include:

  • Nairobi
  • Oxford
  • Kakuma Refugee Camp
  • Kampala
  • Kuala Lumpur
  • Oxfordshire

One of the Hub’s defining features is that the majority of its leadership team has lived experience of displacement, ensuring refugee voices remain central to its mission.

Information Session

Interested applicants can learn more during the online information session.

Date: 6 July 2026

Time: 2:30 PM (UK Time)

The session introduces applicants to:

  • PILnet
  • Fellowship responsibilities
  • Application process
  • Questions and answers

Registration Link:
https://us06web.zoom.us/meeting/register/SBTTM4oOTQqX1Jj6yITpHA

How to Apply

Eligible candidates should prepare their application before the closing date of 15 July 2026.

Interested applicants can learn more and apply for the PILnet-RLRH Fellowship 2026 HERE.

Explore more global job opportunities on OFY here.

The post PILnet-RLRH Fellowship 2026: Fully Remote International Legal Fellowship Supporting Refugee Leaders | Up to 12 Months of Professional Development and Public Interest Law Experience appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department – Lead Climate and Sustainable Development Across 57 Member Countries
item.dc:creator: Grace Amo
item.description:
The Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) is seeking an experienced and visionary leader to serve as the Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department, based at its headquarters in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia. This senior executive position offers a unique opportunity to shape climate action, resilience, disaster risk management, environmental sustainability, women and youth empowerment, and social development […]

The post Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department – Lead Climate and Sustainable Development Across 57 Member Countries appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) is seeking an experienced and visionary leader to serve as the Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department, based at its headquarters in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.

This senior executive position offers a unique opportunity to shape climate action, resilience, disaster risk management, environmental sustainability, women and youth empowerment, and social development initiatives across the Bank’s 57 Member Countries (MCs).

Reporting directly to the Director-General, Global Practices and Partnerships, the successful candidate will provide strategic leadership, oversee multidisciplinary teams, strengthen global partnerships, and guide the implementation of innovative development programmes that advance sustainable and resilient development.

About the Role

The Director will lead the Resilience & Climate Action Department (RCA), providing strategic oversight across several thematic areas critical to IsDB’s development agenda.

The department’s work covers:

  • Climate Change
  • Environment and Social Safards
  • Fragility, Conflict and Resilience (FCR)
  • Disaster Risk Management
  • Emergency Relief
  • Civil Society and NGO Engagement
  • Women’s Empowerment
  • Youth Development

The Director will oversee the following divisions:

  • Climate Change & Environment Division
  • Fragility & Resilience Division
  • Women & Youth Empowerment Division

Key Responsibilities

Strategic Leadership

The Director will:

  • Develop and implement departmental strategies aligned with the IsDB Corporate Strategy.
  • Lead annual and medium-term planning.
  • Establish departmental priorities and performance indicators.
  • Guide implementation of climate resilience and sustainability initiatives.
  • Provide strategic advice to senior management.

Policy Development

Responsibilities include:

  • Leading policy development across resilience and climate action.
  • Supporting Member Country Partnership Strategies.
  • Preparing policy briefs and strategic guidance.
  • Monitoring global developments in sustainability and resilience.
  • Integrating cross-cutting themes into Bank operations.

Climate and Resilience Programming

The successful candidate will oversee programmes focused on:

  • Climate adaptation and mitigation.
  • Environmental sustainability.
  • Disaster preparedness.
  • Emergency response.
  • Fragility and conflict recovery.
  • Capacity development.
  • Women and youth inclusion.
  • Civil society partnerships.

Partnership Development

The Director will strengthen relationships with:

  • Governments.
  • International development partners.
  • NGOs.
  • Civil society organizations.
  • Multilateral institutions.
  • Internal IsDB departments.

The role also includes representing IsDB at international conferences and high-level forums.

Leadership and People Management

The successful candidate will:

  • Lead multidisciplinary teams.
  • Coach and mentor senior managers.
  • Build organizational capability.
  • Drive performance management.
  • Foster collaboration across departments.
  • Support recruitment and talent development.

Operational Oversight

The Director will also:

  • Monitor departmental performance.
  • Oversee budgets and resource allocation.
  • Improve operational processes.
  • Develop monitoring frameworks.
  • Coordinate major Bank-wide resilience initiatives.
  • Support sustainable finance programmes, including Sukuk initiatives.

Required Skills

The ideal candidate should demonstrate expertise in:

  • Climate Change
  • Environmental Sustainability
  • Disaster Risk Management
  • Fragility, Conflict and Resilience
  • Women’s Empowerment
  • Youth Development
  • Civil Society Engagement
  • Sustainable Development
  • Project Design
  • Programme Management
  • Policy Development
  • Strategic Planning
  • Partnership Building
  • Economic Analysis
  • Risk Management
  • Organizational Leadership

Strong leadership, integrity, stakeholder management, communication, and decision-making skills are essential.

Educational Requirements

Applicants must possess:

  • A Master’s degree or PhD in Environmental Sciences, Social Sciences, Conflict and Development, Social Development, Economics, or a related field.

Professional Experience

Candidates should have:

  • At least 15 years of relevant professional experience.
  • A minimum of 7 years in leadership or supervisory roles.
  • At least 5 years working within a development institution.
  • Proven experience leading large multidisciplinary teams.
  • Demonstrated success delivering complex international development programmes.

Why Join the Islamic Development Bank?

Working with IsDB offers an opportunity to contribute to transformative development across 57 Member Countries, supporting projects that address climate resilience, sustainable development, poverty reduction, and social inclusion.

As one of the world’s leading multilateral development finance institutions, IsDB maintains a AAA credit rating and partners with governments, development agencies, and international organizations to deliver high-impact programmes aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

This leadership role allows experienced professionals to influence policies, shape global partnerships, and deliver solutions that improve lives across Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America, and the Middle East.

Required Application Documents

Applicants should prepare:

  • Updated Resume/CV.
  • Copy of Passport.
  • Academic Certificates.

Apply Here 

Application Deadline

Closing Date: 30 July 2026

Applicants are encouraged to submit their applications before the deadline.

Conclusion

The Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department position at the Islamic Development Bank is an outstanding opportunity for experienced international development leaders passionate about climate action, resilience, sustainable development, and inclusive growth.

Leading strategic initiatives across 57 member countries, the successful candidate will play a critical role in advancing climate resilience, strengthening partnerships, and delivering impactful programmes that improve lives and support sustainable development worldwide.

Professionals with extensive leadership experience in international development and climate-related sectors are encouraged to apply before 30 July 2026.

For more information on this opportunity, visit here

Discover more job opportunities on OFY, visit here 

The post Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department – Lead Climate and Sustainable Development Across 57 Member Countries appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026: Global Call for Proposals to Build Digital Solutions for Governments
item.dc:creator: Grace Amo
item.description:
The World Bank, in partnership with the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) and Trust Valley, has launched the GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026, inviting innovative companies from around the world to develop cutting-edge digital solutions that transform public administration in emerging markets. This global Call for Proposals connects innovative businesses with government institutions seeking […]

The post World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026: Global Call for Proposals to Build Digital Solutions for Governments appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The World Bank, in partnership with the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) and Trust Valley, has launched the GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026, inviting innovative companies from around the world to develop cutting-edge digital solutions that transform public administration in emerging markets.

This global Call for Proposals connects innovative businesses with government institutions seeking technology-driven solutions to improve public services, strengthen transparency, modernize public administration, and accelerate digital transformation.

Selected innovators will participate in a fully funded programme that includes executive-level capacity building, a bootcamp in Switzerland, and the opportunity to work directly with government institutions to develop and demonstrate a Proof of Concept (PoC).

About the GovTech Innovation Challenge

Governments worldwide are increasingly embracing digital technologies to improve service delivery, strengthen governance, increase transparency, and enhance operational efficiency.

The GovTech Innovation Challenge creates a bridge between innovative technology companies and public sector institutions by enabling businesses to co-create solutions that solve real government challenges.

The programme is designed to support digital innovation in emerging markets while providing participating companies with valuable experience in delivering technology solutions to public sector clients.

Programme Partners

The initiative is jointly delivered by three globally recognized organizations:

  • World Bank
  • Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO)
  • Trust Valley

Together, these organizations aim to accelerate public sector innovation through collaboration between governments and technology innovators.

Benefits of Participating

Selected companies gain access to a wide range of opportunities and support.

1. World Bank Partnership

Participants benefit from:

  • Exposure to public sector institutions.
  • Credibility associated with World Bank-backed initiatives.
  • Opportunities to engage with government stakeholders.
  • Increased visibility within the international GovTech ecosystem.

2. Fully Funded Business-to-Government (B2G) Programme

Successful applicants participate in an intensive capacity-building programme covering:

  • Government procurement processes.
  • Public sector collaboration.
  • Regulatory compliance.
  • Scaling GovTech businesses.
  • Working with international development institutions.

3. Executive Bootcamp in Switzerland

Top selected companies attend a bootcamp in Switzerland focused on:

  • Public sector innovation.
  • Business-to-government partnerships.
  • International networking.
  • Developing scalable GovTech solutions.

4. Opportunity to Build a Proof of Concept (PoC)

Participants work directly with beneficiary government institutions to:

  • Design digital solutions.
  • Develop prototypes.
  • Test solution feasibility.
  • Demonstrate real-world impact.

This hands-on collaboration provides valuable practical experience while helping governments solve pressing challenges.

5. Fully Covered Programme Costs

The programme provides all core services at no cost.

Selected participants receive support covering:

  • Programme participation.
  • Travel.
  • Accommodation.
  • Meals during official programme activities.

How the Programme Works

The GovTech Innovation Challenge follows a structured process.

Step 1 – Submit a Proposal

Applicants prepare and submit a proposal outlining:

  • Their technical solution.
  • Innovation approach.
  • Expected impact.
  • Ability to address a specific government challenge.

Step 2 – Business-to-Government Capacity Building

Top applicants join a specialized programme designed to strengthen their ability to work with governments.

Participants receive:

  • Executive training.
  • Technical guidance.
  • GovTech mentorship.
  • International networking opportunities.

Step 3 – Develop a Proof of Concept

Selected companies collaborate directly with the beneficiary government to develop and demonstrate their proposed digital solution.

Current Open GovTech Innovation Challenges

The 2026 programme currently includes several innovation challenges from Morocco.

Morocco – General Inspectorate of Finance (Inspection Générale des Finances – IGF)

Challenge:

Modernize:

  • Audit mission programming.
  • Public procurement auditing.

Morocco – Supreme Audit Institution (Cour des Comptes – CDC)

Challenge:

Develop solutions that automate:

  • Public procurement auditing.
  • Compliance verification.
  • Ethics monitoring.
  • Transparency in public procurement.

These challenges seek innovative digital technologies capable of improving accountability and public financial management.

Previous Challenge Example

The programme previously supported the Ghana Revenue Authority (GRA) challenge focused on:

  • Improving tax resource mobilization.
  • Strengthening digital tax administration.
  • Enhancing government revenue systems.

Although the Ghana challenge is now closed, it demonstrates the programme’s commitment to solving practical public sector challenges across emerging economies.

Why Companies Should Apply

Participating organizations can:

  • Expand into the government technology market.
  • Validate innovative products.
  • Collaborate with government institutions.
  • Gain international recognition.
  • Build partnerships with the World Bank ecosystem.
  • Improve product credibility through real-world implementation.
  • Access global GovTech networks.

Who Is This Programme Best Suited For?

The programme is ideal for organizations developing:

  • Artificial Intelligence solutions.
  • Digital audit systems.
  • Public procurement platforms.
  • Tax technologies.
  • Data analytics tools.
  • Automation software.
  • Digital governance platforms.
  • Regulatory technology solutions.
  • Digital identity systems.
  • Government management software.

Application Process

Interested companies should:

  1. Explore the available government challenges.
  2. Select a challenge aligned with their expertise.
  3. Prepare a proposal explaining their technical solution and expected impact.
  4. Submit the proposal through the official programme platform.
  5. Await evaluation and selection.

Successful applicants will be invited to join the Business-to-Government Programme before working directly with government partners on developing their Proof of Concept.

Apply Here

Application Deadline

The programme features multiple open calls, each with its own submission timeline.

Applicants should review the specific challenge page for the applicable deadline before submitting their proposal.

Conclusion

The World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026 provides an outstanding opportunity for innovative companies to collaborate with governments, develop impactful digital solutions, and gain international exposure.

Through funding support, executive training, direct government engagement, and fully covered programme participation, the initiative enables innovators to transform public administration while expanding their presence in the global GovTech ecosystem.

Technology companies seeking to create meaningful public sector impact are encouraged to submit proposals for one of the current open challenges.

For more information on this opportunity, visit here 

Discover more global opportunities on OFY, here 

The post World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026: Global Call for Proposals to Build Digital Solutions for Governments appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: World Sustainability Award 2027: $100,000 Prize Honouring Groundbreaking Research and Transformative Contributions to Sustainability
item.dc:creator: Grace Amo
item.description:
The World Sustainability Award 2027, presented by the MDPI Sustainability Foundation, is a prestigious international recognition celebrating researchers whose groundbreaking work has contributed significantly to advancing sustainability and creating positive global impact. The award recognizes exceptional scholars whose research has demonstrated transformative contributions to sustainability challenges, whether through broad sustainability innovation or through solutions addressing […]

The post World Sustainability Award 2027: $100,000 Prize Honouring Groundbreaking Research and Transformative Contributions to Sustainability appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The World Sustainability Award 2027, presented by the MDPI Sustainability Foundation, is a prestigious international recognition celebrating researchers whose groundbreaking work has contributed significantly to advancing sustainability and creating positive global impact.

The award recognizes exceptional scholars whose research has demonstrated transformative contributions to sustainability challenges, whether through broad sustainability innovation or through solutions addressing specific sustainability-related issues.

The selected recipient will receive a USD $100,000 monetary prize, recognizing outstanding academic excellence, scientific innovation, and societal impact in sustainability research.

The award will be officially presented during the World Sustainability Forum 2027, bringing together global experts, researchers, policymakers, and sustainability leaders committed to addressing pressing environmental and societal challenges.

About the World Sustainability Award

The World Sustainability Award celebrates individuals who are shaping the future of sustainability through research, discovery, and innovation.

Sustainability challenges today require interdisciplinary approaches that combine science, technology, policy, and social innovation. Through this award, the MDPI Sustainability Foundation recognizes researchers whose work provides meaningful solutions and contributes to long-term global sustainability.

The award highlights researchers who have demonstrated excellence in areas such as:

  • Developing innovative sustainability solutions.
  • Advancing scientific understanding of environmental challenges.
  • Creating technologies that support sustainable development.
  • Influencing sustainable practices and policies.
  • Addressing complex global challenges through research and collaboration.

Award Prize and Recognition

The winner of the 2027 World Sustainability Award will receive:

USD $100,000 Research Recognition Prize

The monetary award recognizes the recipient’s outstanding achievements and contributions to sustainability research.

The prize represents:

  • International recognition of scholarly excellence.
  • Appreciation for impactful sustainability research.
  • Support for continued advancement of sustainability knowledge.
  • Recognition among leading global sustainability experts.

Who Should Be Considered for the Award?

The award is designed for researchers whose work has achieved exceptional impact in sustainability.

Potential nominees may include researchers who have:

  • Produced influential sustainability research.
  • Developed innovative approaches to environmental or social challenges.
  • Created solutions with measurable global or community-level impact.
  • Advanced sustainability knowledge across disciplines.
  • Contributed to improving the relationship between human development and environmental protection.

The award welcomes contributions from diverse sustainability fields, recognizing that meaningful solutions often emerge from collaboration across scientific disciplines.

Areas of Research and Impact Recognized

The World Sustainability Award may recognize research contributions related to:

1. Environmental Sustainability

Including work focused on:

  • Climate change mitigation and adaptation.
  • Ecosystem protection.
  • Biodiversity conservation.
  • Environmental restoration.
  • Sustainable resource management.

2. Sustainable Technologies and Innovation

Including:

  • Renewable energy solutions.
  • Green technologies.
  • Sustainable materials.
  • Low-carbon innovations.
  • Technologies supporting environmental protection.

3. Sustainable Development

Including research addressing:

  • Sustainable cities.
  • Responsible production and consumption.
  • Social sustainability.
  • Economic resilience.
  • Inclusive development approaches.

4. Food and Agricultural Sustainability

Including contributions related to:

  • Sustainable farming systems.
  • Climate-resilient agriculture.
  • Food security.
  • Sustainable supply chains.
  • Responsible use of natural resources.

Nomination Process for the World Sustainability Award 2027

The nomination process will officially open on:

Wednesday, 1 July 2026

Candidates must be nominated by a qualified third party through the official online nomination platform.

The nomination process includes:

  1. Submission of a nomination by an eligible third party.
  2. Review of submitted nominations based on the award criteria.
  3. Assessment by an international Selection Committee.
  4. Selection of the award recipient.
  5. Official announcement and award presentation during the World Sustainability Forum 2027.

Important Nomination Requirement

Unlike some awards, the World Sustainability Award does not accept direct applications from researchers.

Researchers interested in being considered must:

  • Be nominated by another qualified individual or organization.
  • Ensure their achievements and research impact are clearly presented.
  • Provide evidence demonstrating the significance of their sustainability contributions.

Selection Process

After the nomination deadline, all eligible submissions will be reviewed by the Selection Committee.

The committee consists of internationally recognized sustainability experts who evaluate nominees based on:

  • Research excellence.
  • Originality and innovation.
  • Scientific contribution.
  • Societal impact.
  • Contribution to advancing sustainability goals.
  • Long-term influence of the researcher’s work.

The committee will select one outstanding researcher as the recipient of the 2027 award.

Award Ceremony

The selected recipient is expected to attend the official award ceremony held during the:

World Sustainability Forum 2027

The ceremony will provide an opportunity to celebrate sustainability leadership and connect the award recipient with global experts working toward sustainable solutions.

Why the World Sustainability Award Matters

Sustainability challenges such as climate change, resource depletion, environmental degradation, and social inequality require innovative research and global collaboration.

By recognizing outstanding researchers, the World Sustainability Award helps:

  • Promote scientific excellence in sustainability.
  • Encourage groundbreaking research.
  • Highlight solutions with global impact.
  • Inspire future generations of sustainability researchers.
  • Strengthen collaboration among sustainability leaders worldwide.

Timeline and Deadline Information

Nomination Opens:
Wednesday, 1 July 2026

Nomination Deadline:
The official closing date for nominations will be announced through the award’s nomination guidelines.

Award Presentation:
During the World Sustainability Forum 2027.

How to Participate

Researchers, institutions, academic leaders, and sustainability professionals who know exceptional sustainability researchers are encouraged to prepare nominations.

Nomination steps include:

  1. Identify an eligible researcher with outstanding sustainability contributions.
  2. Prepare supporting information highlighting their achievements and impact.
  3. Submit the nomination through the official online platform.
  4. Await review by the international Selection Committee.

Apply Here 

Conclusion

The World Sustainability Award 2027 represents one of the leading global recognitions for researchers creating meaningful solutions to sustainability challenges.

With a USD $100,000 prize, international recognition, and presentation at the World Sustainability Forum 2027, the award provides an opportunity to celebrate scientists and scholars whose research is helping build a more sustainable future.

Researchers whose work has transformed sustainability knowledge, influenced society, or delivered innovative solutions are encouraged to be nominated for this prestigious global award.

For more information on this award, visit here 

Discover more global award opportunities on OFY, here 

The post World Sustainability Award 2027: $100,000 Prize Honouring Groundbreaking Research and Transformative Contributions to Sustainability appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027: Up to $492,000 Research Support for Postdoctoral Scientists Advancing Planetary Astronomy and Exoplanet Discovery
item.dc:creator: Grace Amo
item.description:
The Heising-Simons Foundation 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027 is now accepting applications from outstanding postdoctoral scientists seeking to advance groundbreaking research in planetary astronomy. This prestigious fellowship provides exceptional researchers with the opportunity to establish themselves as independent leaders while exploring some of the most important questions about planets, planetary systems, and worlds beyond our […]

The post 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027: Up to $492,000 Research Support for Postdoctoral Scientists Advancing Planetary Astronomy and Exoplanet Discovery appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Heising-Simons Foundation 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027 is now accepting applications from outstanding postdoctoral scientists seeking to advance groundbreaking research in planetary astronomy. This prestigious fellowship provides exceptional researchers with the opportunity to establish themselves as independent leaders while exploring some of the most important questions about planets, planetary systems, and worlds beyond our solar system.

Established in 2017, the fellowship is named after 51 Pegasi b, the first confirmed exoplanet discovered orbiting a Sun-like star. The discovery transformed modern astronomy by opening a new era of research into planets beyond our solar system and how planetary systems form, evolve, and potentially support life.

The fellowship supports scientists whose research contributes to expanding humanity’s understanding of planetary environments, astronomical technologies, and the processes that shape planets both within and beyond our solar system.

About the 51 Pegasi b Fellowship

The 51 Pegasi b Fellowship is designed to support emerging leaders in planetary astronomy by providing funding, mentorship, networking opportunities, and the independence needed to conduct ambitious scientific research.

The fellowship supports research across multiple areas, including:

  • Planet formation and evolution.
  • Exoplanet discovery and characterization.
  • Planetary system dynamics.
  • Astronomical observation techniques.
  • Development of technologies for detecting distant worlds.
  • Theoretical modeling of planetary environments.
  • Experimental approaches that improve understanding of planets and their systems.

Through this fellowship, researchers gain the resources needed to pursue innovative ideas that can create significant advancements in planetary science and astronomy.

What the Fellowship Provides

Successful fellows receive comprehensive support designed to accelerate their scientific careers and research impact.

1. Up to $492,000 in Research Funding

Selected fellows receive up to:

  • $492,000 in financial support over three years.
  • The possibility of applying for an additional fourth year of funding, subject to satisfactory research progress.

This funding enables fellows to dedicate time and resources toward independent research projects, including scientific investigations, data analysis, technological development, and collaboration.

2. Opportunity to Establish Scientific Leadership

The fellowship provides researchers with:

  • Time to focus on high-impact scientific questions.
  • The independence required to develop their own research identity.
  • The opportunity to build recognition as emerging leaders in planetary astronomy.
  • Support to pursue innovative and potentially transformative research directions.

3. Mentorship From Leading Scientists

Each fellow receives guidance and mentorship from an established faculty member at their host institution.

Mentorship support helps fellows:

  • Strengthen their research strategy.
  • Build professional networks.
  • Develop leadership skills.
  • Navigate career advancement opportunities in academia and scientific research.

4. Annual Fellowship Summit and Networking Opportunities

Fellows participate in an annual summit designed to encourage:

  • Professional networking.
  • Scientific collaboration.
  • Exchange of research ideas.
  • Knowledge sharing among leading researchers.
  • Development of long-term partnerships within the planetary astronomy community.

The summit creates a platform for fellows to connect with experts and other researchers working on innovative discoveries.

Why the 51 Pegasi b Fellowship Matters

Planetary astronomy is one of the fastest-growing areas of modern science. Researchers in this field are helping answer fundamental questions about:

  • How planets are created.
  • Why planetary systems develop differently.
  • How planets interact with their environments.
  • Whether other worlds may contain conditions suitable for life.
  • How new technologies can improve the search for distant planets.

By supporting talented scientists, the fellowship contributes to discoveries that expand knowledge of the universe and strengthen the future of astronomy.

Commitment to Diversity and Inclusion

The Heising-Simons Foundation encourages applications from researchers with diverse backgrounds and perspectives.

The fellowship particularly welcomes scientists whose experiences, identities, or career paths are underrepresented in planetary astronomy and who bring innovative ideas capable of creating meaningful contributions to the field.

The foundation believes that diverse scientific communities strengthen discovery, creativity, and collaboration.

Application Information for the 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027

The application period for the 2026/2027 51 Pegasi b Fellowship is currently open.

Applicants are required to submit:

  • An online application form.
  • Two letters of recommendation.
  • Supporting materials required under the official application guidelines.

Applicants should carefully review the eligibility requirements, evaluation criteria, and submission instructions before applying.

Deadline for Application

Application Deadline:
October 1, 2026, at 2:00 PM Pacific Time

Applications submitted after the deadline may not be considered.

How Applications Are Evaluated

Applicants are assessed based on factors including:

  • The quality and originality of their proposed research.
  • Potential contribution to planetary astronomy.
  • Scientific creativity and innovation.
  • Research experience and achievements.
  • Ability to conduct independent research.
  • Potential for leadership and long-term impact in the field.

Research Areas Supported by the Fellowship

The fellowship supports scientists working in areas such as:

  • Exoplanet science.
  • Planetary formation and evolution.
  • Stellar and planetary systems.
  • Astronomical instrumentation.
  • Observational astronomy.
  • Computational planetary science.
  • Experimental planetary research.
  • Methods for detecting and studying distant worlds.

How to Apply

Interested researchers should:

  1. Review the official fellowship eligibility requirements.
  2. Prepare their research proposal and supporting documents.
  3. Secure required recommendation letters.
  4. Complete and submit the online application before the deadline.
  5. Monitor communication from the fellowship team regarding application updates.

For questions and additional information, applicants may contact the Heising-Simons Foundation science team at science@hsfoundation.org.

Apply Here 

Conclusion

The 51 Pegasi b Fellowship represents a significant opportunity for postdoctoral scientists passionate about exploring planets, exoplanets, and the mysteries of planetary systems.

With substantial research funding, expert mentorship, and a global scientific network, the fellowship provides emerging researchers with the platform needed to make meaningful contributions to planetary astronomy and shape the future of space science.

Researchers interested in advancing humanity’s understanding of worlds beyond Earth are encouraged to apply before the October 1, 2026 deadline.

For more information on this fellowship opportunity, visit here 

For more fellowship opportunities on OFY, click here

The post 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027: Up to $492,000 Research Support for Postdoctoral Scientists Advancing Planetary Astronomy and Exoplanet Discovery appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: NCWorks Workforce Pell Grant Program Opens Applications for Eligible Short-Term Workforce Training Providers in North Carolina
item.dc:creator: Grace Amo
item.description:
The NCWorks Commission, in partnership with Governor Josh Stein and the North Carolina Department of Commerce, has officially opened applications for eligible training providers seeking approval to participate in the new Workforce Pell Grant Program. This landmark initiative expands access to federal Pell Grant financial aid for students enrolled in approved short-term workforce training programs. […]

The post NCWorks Workforce Pell Grant Program Opens Applications for Eligible Short-Term Workforce Training Providers in North Carolina appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The NCWorks Commission, in partnership with Governor Josh Stein and the North Carolina Department of Commerce, has officially opened applications for eligible training providers seeking approval to participate in the new Workforce Pell Grant Program.

This landmark initiative expands access to federal Pell Grant financial aid for students enrolled in approved short-term workforce training programs. By making federal funding available for high-quality, career-focused education, the program aims to strengthen North Carolina’s workforce while helping residents acquire in-demand skills for today’s labor market.

The Workforce Pell initiative represents a significant advancement in workforce development by creating new pathways to industry-recognized credentials, certificates, and career opportunities in high-demand sectors across the state.

Application Timeline

Applications for eligible training providers are now open.

Institutions interested in participating should begin the application process as soon as possible.

The NCWorks Commission reviews Workforce Pell applications quarterly.

What Is the Workforce Pell Grant Program?

The Workforce Pell Grant Program expands eligibility for Federal Pell Grants to students enrolled in approved short-term workforce education programs.

Traditionally, Pell Grants have primarily supported students pursuing longer academic programs. Under Workforce Pell, eligible learners can now receive federal financial aid while completing shorter, skills-based training programs designed to prepare them for immediate employment.

The initiative helps reduce financial barriers for students while providing employers with a stronger pipeline of skilled workers.

Governor Josh Stein emphasized that as North Carolina continues attracting record levels of business investment and job creation, Workforce Pell will ensure residents are prepared to take advantage of these opportunities.

Why Workforce Pell Matters

North Carolina continues to experience unprecedented economic growth.

Since taking office in January 2025, Governor Josh Stein has announced:

  • More than 42,000 new jobs
  • Over $29 billion in new business investments
  • Recognition as the Top State for Workforce Development by Site Selection Magazine in January 2026

The Workforce Pell Grant Program directly supports these economic achievements by expanding access to workforce education and helping students gain industry-valued skills.

Objectives of the Program

The Workforce Pell initiative seeks to:

  • Expand access to affordable workforce education
  • Increase enrollment in short-term career training
  • Address employer workforce shortages
  • Support high-demand industries
  • Strengthen North Carolina’s talent pipeline
  • Increase access to industry-recognized credentials
  • Improve career pathways for residents
  • Support economic competitiveness

Who Can Apply?

Applications are currently being accepted from eligible education and training providers, not individual students.

Institutions wishing to participate must first determine whether their workforce training programs satisfy federal Workforce Pell requirements.

Workforce Pell Eligibility Requirements

Eligible training programs must satisfy several federal criteria, including:

  • Last at least eight weeks
  • Last fewer than fifteen weeks
  • Align with high-skill, high-wage, or in-demand industries
  • Count toward academic credit
  • Lead to a certificate or degree pathway
  • Meet all U.S. Department of Education Workforce Pell requirements

Programs meeting these criteria may proceed through the North Carolina state approval process.

Application Process

Eligible institutions should follow these steps:

Step 1

Review each workforce training program to confirm compliance with federal Workforce Pell requirements.

Step 2

Complete the Eligible Training Provider (ETP) application.

Step 3

Submit the additional documentation required for Workforce Pell approval.

Step 4

The NCWorks Commission conducts quarterly reviews of submitted applications.

Step 5

Upon receiving state approval, institutions may submit their eligible programs to the U.S. Department of Education for final federal approval.

Apply Here

State Approval Process

The NCWorks Commission serves as North Carolina’s lead agency for Workforce Pell implementation.

The Commission adopted the state’s Workforce Pell approval policy in May 2026 following the release of final federal regulations by the U.S. Department of Education.

The review process ensures participating institutions meet both federal and state quality standards.

Governor’s Investment in Workforce Development

Governor Josh Stein has made workforce development a central priority of his administration.

To support implementation of Workforce Pell, the Governor’s proposed 2026–2027 budget includes more than $144,000 to establish a dedicated Workforce Pell Director within the North Carolina Department of Commerce.

If approved by the North Carolina General Assembly, the Workforce Pell Director will:

  • Coordinate statewide implementation
  • Support institutional approval processes
  • Collaborate with workforce partners
  • Expand access to approved workforce training programs

Key Partners

Development of North Carolina’s Workforce Pell policy involved collaboration among numerous education and workforce stakeholders through the Governor’s Council on Workforce and Apprenticeships.

Key partners include:

  • NCWorks Commission
  • North Carolina Department of Commerce
  • North Carolina Community College System
  • University of North Carolina System
  • North Carolina Independent Colleges and Universities
  • Governor’s Council on Workforce and Apprenticeships
  • U.S. Department of Education

Supporting North Carolina’s Economic Development Strategy

Workforce Pell also advances the state’s broader First in Opportunity Strategic Economic Development Plan, which emphasizes:

  • Building a resilient workforce
  • Preparing workers for future industries
  • Expanding access to valuable credentials
  • Strengthening employment pathways
  • Supporting employer workforce needs

Funding

The NCWorks Commission is funded through the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA) Title I, supported entirely by the Employment and Training Administration of the U.S. Department of Labor, totaling $66,329,591.

Benefits of Workforce Pell

Participating institutions and future students stand to benefit through:

  • Expanded federal financial aid access
  • Increased workforce training enrollment
  • Stronger employer partnerships
  • Improved workforce readiness
  • Support for high-demand industries
  • Enhanced credential attainment
  • Greater career opportunities
  • Increased economic mobility

Why Eligible Institutions Should Apply

Workforce Pell creates an unprecedented opportunity for colleges and workforce training providers to offer federally funded short-term education that aligns directly with employer needs.

By becoming an approved Workforce Pell provider, institutions can attract more students, expand access to career-focused education, and contribute to North Carolina’s rapidly growing economy.

For more information on this grant visit here 

For more grant opportunities on OFY, click here

The post NCWorks Workforce Pell Grant Program Opens Applications for Eligible Short-Term Workforce Training Providers in North Carolina appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: CSC Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27: Funding Opportunity for Commonwealth Alumni to Create Community Impact
item.dc:creator: Phionah Tumusiime
item.description:
Commonwealth Alumni with innovative ideas for community development projects can now apply for the Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27, an annual funding opportunity designed to support alumni-led activities that promote knowledge sharing, awareness creation, and practical solutions to development challenges. The fund supports Commonwealth Alumni who want to transform ideas into meaningful community action […]

The post CSC Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27: Funding Opportunity for Commonwealth Alumni to Create Community Impact appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Commonwealth Alumni with innovative ideas for community development projects can now apply for the Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27, an annual funding opportunity designed to support alumni-led activities that promote knowledge sharing, awareness creation, and practical solutions to development challenges.

The fund supports Commonwealth Alumni who want to transform ideas into meaningful community action through workshops, training sessions, community forums, ideathons, civic engagement projects, and other collaborative activities.

The Commonwealth Scholarship Commission (CSC) created ACEF to recognise that lasting change often begins at the community level. By supporting alumni-led initiatives, the programme enables individuals and groups to share knowledge, build networks, and encourage practical action on important global development issues.

The application deadline is:

Sunday, 26 July 2026 at 23:59 BST

About the CSC Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF)

The Alumni Community Engagement Fund is an annual initiative of the Commonwealth Scholarship Commission designed to support Commonwealth Alumni in creating community-based engagement activities.

The programme focuses on empowering alumni to become active contributors to development by using their skills, knowledge, and networks to address challenges within their communities.

ACEF provides financial support for activities that encourage:

  • Awareness raising
  • Knowledge exchange
  • Community dialogue
  • Skills development
  • Collaborative problem-solving
  • Practical action

The fund recognises that small, locally driven initiatives can create significant long-term impact when communities are equipped with knowledge, resources, and opportunities to work together.

Purpose of the ACEF Programme

The main purpose of ACEF is to help Commonwealth Alumni design and deliver activities that respond to important development issues while strengthening community participation.

The programme encourages alumni to move beyond discussions and create practical solutions that benefit their communities.

Supported activities should aim to:

  • Educate communities about key development challenges.
  • Encourage conversations and collaboration.
  • Share professional and academic knowledge.
  • Build community capacity.
  • Inspire individuals and organisations to take action.

2026-27 ACEF Priority Themes

For the 2026-27 funding cycle, applicants are invited to submit projects addressing one of three priority themes.

Applicants must choose only one theme when applying.

1. Artificial Intelligence for Development

This theme supports initiatives exploring how artificial intelligence can contribute to sustainable development and community progress.

Potential activities may focus on:

  • Raising awareness about AI opportunities and challenges.
  • Building digital skills.
  • Exploring responsible AI use.
  • Encouraging innovation through technology.
  • Helping communities understand how AI can support development goals.

2. Strengthening Blue Economies in Small Island Developing States (SIDS)

This theme focuses on supporting sustainable economic opportunities connected to marine and coastal resources.

Projects may explore:

  • Sustainable use of ocean resources.
  • Coastal community development.
  • Marine conservation.
  • Innovation within blue economies.
  • Climate resilience for island communities.

Important eligibility requirement:

Applicants applying under this theme must be living or working in a Commonwealth Small Island Developing State (SIDS).

3. Women Entrepreneurs: Catalyst for Change

This theme focuses on supporting women entrepreneurs and recognising their role in economic and social transformation.

Activities may address:

  • Entrepreneurship skills development.
  • Business knowledge sharing.
  • Women’s economic empowerment.
  • Innovation and leadership.
  • Creating supportive networks for women entrepreneurs.

Types of Activities ACEF Can Support

Applicants can propose a wide range of community engagement activities.

Examples include:

Skills-Based Training

Projects may include:

  • Masterclasses
  • Training workshops
  • Professional development sessions
  • Capacity-building activities

Educational Events

Examples include:

  • Lectures
  • Seminars
  • Knowledge-sharing sessions
  • Community learning events

Civic Engagement Projects

Applicants may design activities that:

  • Encourage community participation.
  • Promote awareness of development issues.
  • Support collective action.

Community Forums

Activities may involve:
